county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Hill and Teton counties. Named for Montana Governor Joseph K. Toole.
county_metadata.research_tips: Toole County Clerk and Recorder in Shelby has records from 1914. Shelby is famous for hosting the Jack Dempsey-Tommy Gibbons heavyweight championship fight in 1923 — the event nearly bankrupted the town, and financial records at MHS document the civic involvement. The Kevin-Sunburst oil field (1922+) brought oil workers; Montana Oil and Gas Conservation Division records document operators and landowners. Check Hill and Teton counties for pre-1914 records. The Marias Museum of History and Art in Shelby has local family collections. The Great Northern Railway Hi-Line brought homesteaders. The Sweetgrass port of entry on the Canadian border generated customs and immigration records at NARA. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries. The Shelby Promoter newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1914 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1914. County Clerk and Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1943.

source_profiles.source_name: Toole County Birth and Death Records

source_profiles.coverage_notes: State registration from 1907; county-filed records may predate 1907. Certified copies from Montana DPHHS Vital Records. Some counties have earlier records filed with the county clerk.
Civil and criminal case files, probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, and name changes from 1914.
Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, and probate. Browse-only image collections from Toole County Clerk and Recorder's office.
Federal population schedules for Toole County. Montana Territory enumerated from 1870. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide additional family details.
Historical newspapers from Toole County communities. Montana Historical Society Newspaper Archives and Chronicling America have digitized Montana papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Rosebud County. One of Montana's smallest counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Treasure County Clerk and Recorder in Hysham has records from 1919. Treasure County is among Montana's least-populated counties. The Yellowstone River valley attracted irrigated farmers and ranchers. Check Rosebud County for pre-1919 records. The Treasure County 89'ers Museum in Hysham preserves homestead-era records and family photographs. BLM's GLO records document original homestead patents along the Yellowstone valley. The Northern Pacific Railroad ran through the county bringing settlers and providing economic connection. Irrigation district records document water rights holders. Livestock brand records at the Montana Department of Livestock identify ranching families. The Hysham Echo newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. The extremely small population means court records, school censuses, and church registers provide near-complete family coverage.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Dawson County. Named for the Missouri River valley. Home to Fort Peck Dam.
county_metadata.research_tips: Valley County Clerk and Recorder in Glasgow has records from 1893. Fort Peck Dam construction (1933-1940) brought 10,000+ workers to the area — Army Corps of Engineers records at NARA document construction workers and their families, including worker registration cards, accident reports, and payroll records. The Valley County Pioneer Museum in Glasgow has local family files. Glasgow Air Force Base (1957-1968) military personnel records at NARA. Check Dawson County for pre-1893 records. The Great Northern Railway brought homesteaders along the Hi-Line; railroad land records at the Minnesota Historical Society. The Missouri River steamboat era (pre-railroad) brought early traders; fur company records at MHS. The Glasgow Courier newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from Meagher and Sweet Grass counties. Named for wheat farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wheatland County Clerk and Recorder in Harlowton has records from 1917. Harlowton was a Milwaukee Road railroad town — the railroad's electrification through the Rocky Mountains (1915-1974) made Harlowton a division point, and railroad employment records at MHS identify workers and their families. The Upper Musselshell Museum in Harlowton has local family files. Check Meagher and Sweet Grass counties for pre-1917 records. Homesteaders arrived with the railroad in the early 1900s; many claims were abandoned during drought. BLM's GLO records document original homestead patents. The Wheatland County newspaper (the Harlowton Times) archives are on microfilm at MHS. Judith Gap and Two Dot were small railroad communities with distinct settlement histories. Some Czech and Norwegian immigrant families settled in the county.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Dawson and Fallon counties. Named for Pierre Wibaux, French cattle baron.
county_metadata.research_tips: Wibaux County Clerk and Recorder in Wibaux has records from 1914. Pierre Wibaux was a French nobleman who built one of Montana's largest cattle empires in the 1880s-90s — Wibaux family records and business correspondence at MHS document the ranching community. The Pierre Wibaux Museum in Wibaux has local family files and ranching artifacts. Check Dawson and Fallon counties for pre-1914 records. The Northern Pacific Railroad brought homesteaders; railroad land records at MHS and the Minnesota Historical Society. BLM's GLO records document homestead patents. Wibaux County's tiny population (consistently under 2,000) means that every family appears in county records, newspapers, and school censuses. The Wibaux Pioneer-Gazette newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. Oil exploration in the Williston Basin brought workers in the mid-20th century.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1883 from Custer and Gallatin counties. Montana's most populous county. Billings is the largest city in Montana.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yellowstone County Clerk and Recorder in Billings holds deed records from 1883. Billings was founded in 1882 as a Northern Pacific Railroad town named for railroad president Frederick Billings. The Western Heritage Center in Billings has extensive genealogy collections including family files, oral histories, and ethnic community records. The Parmly Billings Library genealogy room has surname indexes, city directories, and school yearbooks. Billings became Montana's commercial hub — business records, chamber of commerce files, and professional directories at MHS identify residents. The Billings Gazette newspaper (1885+) archives are on microfilm. Yellowstone County attracted diverse settlers including German-Russian farmers in the sugar beet areas, and railroad workers from across Europe. The Yellowstone Art Museum and Moss Mansion preserve Gilded Age history. Check Custer and Gallatin counties for pre-1883 records.
